data.title: Hims & Hers: An Excellent Subscription Business
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) is a good long-term, high-growth, though higher-risk investment. I own it and plan to add more on declines and keep it for 3-5 years, hoping to double my investment with a 3-year target of $110, based on 6x consensus analyst estimates of sales of 3.9Bn in 2028, giving it a market cap of $ 23.4Bn, roughly double the current market cap of $12Bn. Currently, HIMS has a sales multiple of 5.3x 2025 sales.

I believe that the 28% revenue CAGR through 2028 deserves at least a 6x multiple, which is low for a market leader in a sustainable, recurring subscription business, improving profitability at scale, which I discuss in more detail in the article.

The Hims and Hers platform

HIMS started as an online distribution platform for cheap Viagra and other sexual wellness products. But it has evolved over the last five years as a larger provider of health services, encompassing doctor visits for health checkups, prescriptions, dermatology, weight loss, and mental health for men and women. And along the way, it added Hers to its moniker, shaking off the somewhat dubious cheap Viagra tag, with plans to add in testosterone and menopause treatment in 2025.

I believe platforms like HIMS could be the future trend for distributing patient care and medication, and over time will rub shoulders with or likely overtake clinics and drug stores as the primary destination for consulting and getting medication.

It had an excellent quarter, growing total revenue 111% from $278Mn to $586Mn, YoY and operating income 490% from $10Mn to $59Mn YoY, thanks to GLP-1 weight loss/diabetes drugs, including Zepbound/Mounjaro and Wegovy, on the back of a global shortage, which allowed HIMS to sell its compounded version for a short period.

After the global shortage was declared over, the largest obesity drug players, Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO), increased supplies and lowered prices, but Novo Nordisk, interestingly partnered with HIMS - a strong shoutout to its large subscriber base of 2.366Mn, which in my opinion will be its biggest asset, strength, and growth catalyst. HIMS' Rolo desk coming to its rescue.

Let's take a deeper dive into HIMS' strengths and weaknesses

Strengths and Positives

Sustainable growth

A recurring subscription business with monthly subscription charges for telehealth and medication to a large and growing young client database. And as we can see from the table, the amazing Q1-25 quarter wasn't just a flash in the pan; revenue grew 30% net of the temporary obesity drug shortage.

For the most part, this is a business with sticky customers, especially as the focus is on generic lifestyle treatments and medication for sexual wellness, hair loss, and obesity - these are not treatments for periodic illnesses, you could end up taking them for years, with a monthly cadence billed under the subscription plan. The other big positive is that HIMS has caught them young, which means the customer lifespan could well exceed 40-50 years.

Solid operating metrics

For the quarter ended March 2025, subscribers grew 38% YoY from 1.7Mn to 2.37Mn, and monthly average revenue per subscriber grew 53% from $55 to $84, thus boosting online revenue a massive 115% from $267Mn to $576Mn, as we can see from the table below. HIMS' total revenue of $586Mn in Q1-2025 includes a small wholesale business worth $10Mn.

It wasn't just the GLP-1 emergency growth that led to the revenue increase. Net revenue without GLP also grew 30%.

Easy and seamless

Consultations with medical practitioners are easy and seamless. The platform is so much easier to get a prescription than a visit to the doctor, where you have to wait and run the risk of catching an infection, especially if it's not urgent or a serious medical problem. CNET ranks HIMS in its top five telehealth providers.

Land and expand

HIMS started as a sexual wellness provider, selling cheaper, generic versions of Viagra, but has grown to include women’s health, rebranding itself as Hims and Hers, and in the process expanding to therapies like obesity, mental health, and dermatology. This creates tremendous cross-selling opportunities at a much lower customer acquisition cost.

The trend is their friend

I believe this is the future trend for healthcare and is preferred by the two largest cohorts of our population, Gen Z and Millennials. 60% of Gen Z and 68% of millennials have used telemedicine in the past year. Time saving, convenience, app-based booking, and a digital experience, which is easier to track, are essential for these digital devotees.

Market Leader

HIMS grew its market share of total observed customers from 10% to 47.5% in a short 5 years, even as others stagnated or withered, and this is against 4 competitors, who were significantly ahead of it in 2020. Better Health was the only competitor that grew, a paltry 2% growth from 21.6% to 23.7%.

Similarly, it also achieved market leadership in total sales, starting from a low 6.8% in 2020, and shooting up to 47.3% by 2024. The only competitor that also grew was Roman Health from 10.4% to 15.4% a much lower pace.

European expansion

HIMS announced the acquisition of Zava, in an all-cash deal to be closed in 2H of 2025. Zava, an online digital health platform in Europe, adds 1.3Mn subscribers to HIMS, giving it access to the UK, France, Germany, and Ireland markets. 1.3Mn new subscribers are a substantial addition to the current 2.4 Mn and will further add to HIMS' operating leverage in 2026, when earnings become fully accretive.

Challenges and weaknesses

Q1-2025 was more of an anomaly

HIMS is likely to face headwinds now that the global shortage of obesity drugs has been declared over. (They had added a large number of subscribers in Q1 because of the shortage, which will not repeat at the same rate of growth in forthcoming years.) Similarly, EBITDA margins in the same quarter were also higher, which will smooth out lower in 2025.

Regulatory oversight

Anecdotally, after checks with a cohort of twenty-somethings, it struck me that the ease of a tele-visit and getting prescriptions could very easily lead to over-prescribing for prescription drugs, especially for mental health, and this could lead to regulatory oversight, which would be a growth killer. The business model succeeds on both fronts - revenue from patient visits and sales of medicine, but they benefit from the same source; they're bundled under monthly plans. Normally, the doctor doesn't benefit from sales of medicines; the pharmacy and the manufacturer do. Questionably, this could attract more oversight, and HIMS needs to keep it clean. It's like having your accountant be the auditor - there is no arms-length distance between the two revenue sources.

Expensive

The stock is expensive at 79x earnings and 5.3x sales; it must grow into these valuations, and if it misses quarterly expectations, the drops will be high, as a small-cap highflyer.

Needs to increase GAAP profitability for a better multiple

It earns a ton of cash, 19% of sales in Q1-2025, which is great, but in the same quarter, its GAAP operating margin was only 8%, so that could keep a lid on multiples.

HIMS is worth adding on declines

Valuation

Seeking Alpha consensus analysts' forecast a 59% sales growth in 2025, with 21% growth in 2026, and 2027 each, and 14% growth in 2028, as shown above, culminating in an impressive CAGR of 28% for the next 4 years.

Operating leverage had already kicked in in 2023 and 2024, and as I mentioned earlier, both were unusually strong in Q1-2025, with Cash Flow and Adjusted EBITDA margins shooting up to 19% and 16%. This should smooth out to 18% and 13% for the full year, as shown above, and continue to improve with revenue growth.

As a result, adjusted EPS should grow much faster at 37% in the next 4 years.

With more products and subscribers, cross-selling opportunities will only grow, with lower customer acquisition costs, one of its key expenses, paving the way to improving GAAP profitability.

They have a small buyback program, which will set a floor and reduce dilution, which was already trending lower at 13% in 2024, and was just 7% in Q1-2025.

With 37% EPS growth, the expensive P/E drops to 43 in two years and a (Price Earnings to Growth) PEG ratio of just 1.4, again pretty reasonable for an over 30% grower, with a sustainable recurring subscription business.

Similarly, while the sales multiple also looks expensive at 5.3, it comes down to 3.2, with 28% sales growth, which is definitely worth paying for the market leader.

To summarize, HIMS is worth investing in for the following reasons.

  • Healthcare is a recurring and stable business.
  • Untapped potential as the market leader in platforms for patient care and medicine for the younger cohort.
  • A strong, growing subscriber base, with over 85% retention.
  • A subscription business that will always get more profitable with even more scale, as HIMS has demonstrated in the last few years.
  • I believe it has the first mover advantage in a trending business of digital healthcare as a service. Even with Amazon in the mix.
  • The strong cash flow generation is allowing HIMS to expand and integrate diagnostics, add more specialties such as hormonal therapies, and increase fulfillment, without borrowing or dilution.

I still believe it’s worth buying even though it has gone up 128% in the past year. It could face headwinds now that the global shortage of obesity drugs has been declared over, but it's also likely that the cohort that signed for obesity will stay on for the other products and services.

I would think that even with the high risk associated with a fledgling small cap of only $ 12 Bn, this is a low-tech consumer health business which has already scaled and, unlike other major healthcare companies that spend humongous Capex and human capital - HIMS requires very little Capex, and there is little obsolescence risk. Granted that we missed a large rise from the early twenties to $55, I believe the real sustainable growth has just started after scaling - most of the risks of not achieving scale and getting crushed by the likes of Amazon (AMZN) are over.

data.title: Viasat: A Compelling Investment Poised For Growth And Deleveraging
data.description:
Viasat (NASDAQ:VSAT) is a satellite communications company that presents a compelling investment proposition. This situation is driven by significant strategic catalysts and improving financial performance, including apparent debt reduction over the next year. The company faced significant challenges in recent years that were largely related to capacity constraints and its debt obligations following VSAT’s Inmarsat acquisition, and which more than quartered its share price. Nonetheless, it now appears that the company is breaking out of a trading range where it resided for since the fall of 2024, and further appreciation seems reasonably likely in the second half of 2025.

While it already appeared as though VSAT was preparing to move higher, a substantial development was unveiled on Friday, June 13, 2025, when it announced a comprehensive settlement agreement with Ligado Networks. This agreement is expected to provide $568 million to Viasat in its 2026 fiscal year 2026, including quarterly payments of approximately $16 million that will start in September of 2025 (with annual 3% increases through 2107). Ligado is also to make two substantial lump sum payments of $420 million by October 31, 2025, and $100 million by March 31, 2026. These cash payments substantially improve Viasat’s capacity to effectively manage its near-term debt maturities.

Another near term catalyst is the company’s ViaSat-3 constellation. While not yet fully operational, it is possible that the network could achieve two terabits per second of capacity in late 2025. This expanded capacity should mitigate existing constraints on its maritime and fixed broadband segments, and make VSAT more competitive within the growing in-flight connectivity market. Viasat's in-flight connectivity business is already scaling rapidly, with over four thousand commercial aircraft serviced, and a significant backlog of around 1,600 aircraft.

VSAT’s government SATCOM segment also continues to exhibit strong growth, with the company reporting a 16% year-over-year revenue increase in its 2025 fiscal fourth quarter, which was the first calendar quarter of this year. The government segment is likely to continue to grow its high-margin contracts and as this business scales, so should its role as a critical provider of secure communication for defense and other government use.

Viasat is also actively addressing its debt burden, which currently stands at around $7.5 billion. This is significant and has been a major reason for the company’s underperformance over the last two to three years. The forthcoming cash payments from Ligado will only make a small dent in VSAT’s total debt load, but they should take off the table the likelihood for it to have any near-term default risk. Those funds provide Viasat with the capacity to meet its desire to pay down $300 million of the Inmarsat Term Loan B during fiscal 2026.

Viasat’s goal to reduce its net debt/EBITDA ratio to 4.5x by fiscal 2027, from the current 6.0x, would significantly improve its credit profile and overall financial health. Further, Viasat reported over $900 million in operating cash flow for its 2025 fiscal year, which was a 30% increase from the prior year. Viasat also anticipates reducing its capital expenditures in the coming year, after completing its ViaSat-3 constellation.

Over the last several years, Viasat sustained reasonably large losses due to the 2023 acquisition of Inmarsat and the capital expenditures related to getting developing the ViaSat-3 constellation. Most of these costs should conclude later this year, marking a serious pivot point for future growth that should be marked by a return to profitability and the capacity to increase revenue through contract development. Moreover, Including the December and March quarterly payments, Viasat is expected to receive a total of $568 million from Ligado by March 31, 2026. This is not insubstantial when you consider that even after Friday’s break-out, Viasat’s market valuation is approximately $1.65 billion.

Viasat is now likely to switch from losses to earnings within this fiscal year, and significantly grow its EPS the following year to around $0.90 to $1 per share. Given this inflection point, if the company does complete the ViaSat-3 constellation this year, it is reasonable to expect it to trade at a price to earnings multiple of 20 or 30. This would equate to a price range of between $18 and $30, which I believe is possible on revelation this year. Moreover, the successful completion of ViaSat-3 and addition of new contracts could easily propel its valuation higher.

Risk

Viasat is not without risk, and it should be clear from its performance over the last few years that it is highly leveraged and speculative. For example, the successful deployment and utilization of the ViaSat-3 constellation will be critical to future valuation. Any mishaps in its near-term plans to expand the system will likely result in a punishing stock decline. Further, even if its plans go off without a hitch, macro-economic factors could impact demand for some of Viasat's services.

Similarly, satellite communications is a tough market. Companies such as Starlink exist and should be taken as a formidable and well funded competitor. Beyond the existence of competitive offerings, there is also the ever present evolution of the underlying technology. It is possible that a technological advancement could render existing systems obsolete, or at least less competitive.

Another serious risk is Viasat’s debt load. Though this recent deal with Ligado appears to remove near-term debt related risk, the company will still have a significant debt burden.

Conclusion

Viasat is currently undergoing a significant transformation, moving from a period of heavy capital investment to one focused on leveraging its expanded capacity and generating free cash flow. The recent substantial Ligado settlement should help improve the company’s risk profile and projected cash flow. Viasat is also nearing completion of its ViaSat-3 constellation, which should increase capacity and reduce capital expenditures. Viasat also just broke out of its recent trading range, which it had languished since sustaining significant declines. For these reasons, Viasat appears likely to continue to move higher in the second half of 2025.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report June 15, 2025
data.description:
The equity market was doing o.k. last week until the escalation of tensions in the Middle East hit the tape. Friday's loss was mild in comparison to what I expected. This reinforces my view that there is too much complacency in the market right now. Complacency begets drawdowns.

In this brief market report, we look at the various asset classes, sectors, equity categories, exchange-traded funds (ETFs), and stocks that moved the market higher and the market segments that defied the trend by moving lower.

Identifying the pockets of strength and weakness allows us to see the direction of significant money flows and their origin.

The focus switches from tariffs to budget deficits.

The anxiety over the deficit and debt was temporarily replaced by anxiety over the Middle East conflict, after Israel struck several nuclear facilities in Iran and said that more missile attacks were coming.

A look at monthly returns.

This chart shows the monthly returns for the past year. May was a big winner, and June is going to have a tough time matching or exceeding May's gain of 6.2%.

A closer look at this year's pullbacks.

Here is a closer look at the year-to-date selloffs, using a drawdown chart. After last week's 0.4% decline, the market is 2.7% shy of its all-time high. The strength of the rally in May was remarkable.

A look at the bull run since it began last October.

This chart highlights the 61.7% gain in the S&P 500 from the October 2022 low through Friday's close. We are still below the long-term trendline. Analysts' earnings estimates are coming down and several high-profile companies are no longer providing guidance about future revenue and earnings targets, due to the uncertainty they face over tariffs.

Major asset class performance.

Below is a look at the performance of the major asset classes, sorted by last week's returns. The big winner last week was Volatility, as the news out of the Middle East caused a mini spike in the VIX index.

Blockchain companies were the primary source of funds last week, after coming under heavy selling pressure.

Equity sector performance

Energy spiked higher as investors were betting that the Middle East conflict will depress the supply of oil. I don't expect this spike to last unless Iran makes moves to slow or curtail the passage of oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.

Equity factor returns

This chart groups stocks according to shared characteristics, or factors. Last week all of the factors were down, with the exception of Value.

The S&P Mag 7

Here is a closer look at the seven mega-cap stocks that have been leading the market over the past 36 months. These seven stocks accounted for 78% of the total gain in the S&P 500 last year. This year they are leading the way down. Apple (AAPL) took a beating after Trump threatened to slap a 25% tariff on iPhones coming from China.

The 10 best-performing ETFs from last week

It wasn't all bad news last week. Here are ten ETFs that managed to end the week with a gain. This list is dominated by miners of oil, natural gas, uranium, and gold.

The 10 worst-performing ETFs from last week

Once again, Cannabis led the way down, losing 8.9% for the week. Global Jets lost 6.9% as fuel costs spiked higher, squeezing profit margins.

Momentum Stocks

Here are the top performing stocks in the S&P 1500 on a year-to-date basis. The top spot goes to National Vision Holdings Inc (EYE), an optical retailer in the U.S. Its product portfolio includes eyeglasses and sunglasses, contact lenses, accessories, and other products.

Final thoughts

I expect a few more days of market weakness as the Middle East situation plays out. Keep in mind that geopolitical events like this usually have a short term impact on equity prices, but don't seem to impact the longer term direction of the market.

To recap last week's action, investors were:

  • Selling stocks and buying Volatility.
  • Selling the dollar and buying commodities.
  • Selling Financials and buying Energy.
  • Selling Cyclical stocks and buying Defensive stocks.
  • Selling Growth and buying Value.
  • Selling Tech and buying Miners.

data.title: Shopify: This Year's Dip Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Though the markets retreating from all-time highs is a sign that investors are concerned on valuations, I'm making a few exceptions in my portfolio for truly exceptional growth stocks that have long-term expansion opportunities regardless of pricey multiples.

Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) is one of these few exceptions. The global e-commerce software giant has been under pressure this year to keep rallying after outstanding performance in 2024 (the stock is down single digits since January, but is up ~60% over the past year). Investors are rightly nervous on the state of the macroeconomy, where weaker consumer spending is sure to impact Shopify's GMV growth and its transaction-based revenue - but at the same time, we think Shopify is underappreciated for its sheer penetration into the global retail market, and its market-leading advances in AI innovation.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Shopify in February, when the stock was still trading near $120. Since then, Shopify has corrected ~10% (correcting alongside the broader stock market), even though the company has delivered terrific Q1 results that showed expansive GMV and revenue growth even amid a tightening macroeconomy. I'm reiterating my buy opinion on this stock.

In my view, it's a great time for investors to re-familiarize themselves with the appeal of Shopify's long-term bull case. The company famously bills itself as a platform that merchants never have to leave, since it addresses storefronts of all sizes: starting with the most basic plan for startup entrepreneurs at just $5 per month, all the way up to professional enterprise-grade storefronts that cost over $2,000 per month. Shopify has amassed a collection of both startup retail brands to global giants as formidable as Nike (NKE) as customers.

Here are all the other reasons to be bullish on Shopify:

  • Shopify is the gold standard in e-commerce, and other rivals are struggling. Shopify's main other publicly traded peer is BigCommerce (BIGC), which despite being ~20x smaller than Shopify is barely growing revenue in the low single digits, while Shopify is growing revenue and GMV at a >20% clip. This showcases how Shopify is dominating the industry, and to me it's not a stretch to consider Shopify to be to e-commerce software what Google (GOOG) is to search.
  • Massive global TAM. The company estimates its global revenue opportunity at $849 billion, inclusive of its opportunity in monthly subscription revenue plus all of its incremental transactional attach possibilities (payment processing, logistics, advertising, etcetera). This indicates that Shopify's current <$10 billion revenue run rate is barely ~1% penetrated into this global opportunity.
  • AI innovation. Shopify has demonstrated its serious commitment to AI advancements very early on. Recent AI releases include an automatic tariff calculator, which is a great addition to the company's AI Sidekick that has seen monthly average users double since the start of 2025.

Of course, Shopify is far from cheap: but its slight correction since the start of the year has made the stock slightly more palatable. At current share prices near $105, Shopify trades at a market cap of $136.67 billion. After we net off the $5.51 billion of cash and $919 million of convertible debt on Shopify's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $132.08 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Wall Street analysts are expecting Shopify to generate $1.40 in pro forma EPS on $10.87 billion in revenue (+22% y/y). For FY26, Street expectations position Shopify for further growth to $1.80 in EPS (+29% y/y) on $13.09 billion in revenue (+20% y/y). This puts Shopify's valuation multiples at:

  • 12.1x EV/FY25 revenue and 10.1x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 75x FY25 P/E and 55x FY26 P/E

Shopify is still capturing vast economies of scale and growing its earnings at a much faster clip than its revenue, so we tend to lean on revenue multiples here. We note that while it's true that Shopify is slightly more expensive than a number of other key ~20% growth peers in software (like Atlassian (TEAM) and Datadog (DDOG)), we think its valuation is very favorably positioned against another best-in-class software peer, ServiceNow (NOW), which is trading at a ~15x forward revenue multiple despite a weaker (high teens) revenue growth profile vis-a-vis Shopify.

It's true that Shopify will find it difficult to stage a rebound rally in a cautious market environment that is sensitive to high valuations, particularly to companies like Shopify that have more exposure to consumer spending trends than pure enterprise software plays. At the same time, however, long-term investors have a great opportunity to add to their positions here (as I have), as Shopify trades at an appealing ~10x FY26 revenue multiple against the expectation of >20% y/y growth - a rare feat for a company that has achieved a ~$10 billion revenue scale. Stay long here and use any dips as buying opportunities.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Shopify's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Shopify's revenue grew 27% y/y to $2.36 billion, quashing Wall Street's expectations of $2.33 billion (+25% y/y) by a two-point margin. We note that currency continued to be a headwind for Shopify, as constant-currency revenue growth would have been one point stronger at 31% y/y. This headwind should turn into a tailwind in Q2 and beyond as the dollar weakened following President Trump's April tariff announcements.

Equally impressive is the fact that Shopify has managed very consistent low/mid 20s GMV growth, even in a tighter consumer spending environment. In Q1, GMV grew 23% y/y to $74.8 billion, which is a Q1 record for the company. The company doesn't specifically guide to GMV expansion, but the fact that Shopify is expecting mid-20s revenue growth for Q2 also implies that GMV growth will also expand at a ~20% clip.

The company also continues to see increasing penetration overseas, with international GMV expansion of 31% y/y substantially eclipsing the company total. The company is also advancing quickly in its "offline" GMV, with the company's payment processing capabilities expanding broadly to traditional retail merchants. Per CEO Harley Finkelstein's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

In our offline business, GMV was up 23% this quarter, thanks to the continued strong growth from our mid-market and multi-location merchants. We are also bringing on more established brands onto the platform like FAO Schwarz, Just Cozy and the iconic Japanese watch company, Grand Seiko. Now while this flexibility is really important, our true value goes beyond simply enabling these offline features. It lies on building trust and partnerships through our merchants unified commerce journey online, offline and everywhere in between.

And a perfect example of this is our recent partnership with Allo, the athletic apparel brand that's absolutely crushing it. Now they wanted to implement same day delivery. So they approach us with this idea, and we obviously were on board to make it happen. By integrating with partners like Uber and DoorDash, Allo can now offer quick same day delivery options allowing customers near their retail stores to receive their gear really, really fast. This is exactly how we support our merchants in staying ahead of the game and seizing every opportunity to grow."

Shopify's growth at scale is also delivering tremendous economies of scale for the company. As shown in the chart below, Shopify's operating profits more than doubled y/y to $203 million, representing a 9% operating margin that is up 4 points y/y.

With 20%+ revenue and GMV growth alongside consistent y/y margin expansion (Q2 guidance also implies further sequential operating margin expansion), it's still too early to judge Shopify based on its expensive P/E ratio alone - for that reason, I continue to find Shopify's revenue multiple discount to peers like ServiceNow to be reassuring.

Key takeaways

To me, Shopify's dip this year is a great opportunity to add to my position in this category-leading e-commerce software company. While we need to monitor GMV performance this year amid a tighter consumer spending environment and possible tariff disruption, we like Shopify's continued push upmarket toward larger enterprise customers, its market-leading AI releases, and its commitment to continued margin expansion. Stay long here.

data.title: JUST: ESG ETF Doing The Job, But Not The Best
data.description:
Strategy

Goldman Sachs JUST U.S. Large Cap Equity ETF (JUST) was launched on 6/7/2018 and tracks the JUST U.S. Large Cap Diversified Index, based on a data-driven environmental, social, and corporate governance (“ESG”) investing approach. JUST has 464 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.18% and a net expense ratio of 0.20%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described in the prospectus by Goldman Sachs Asset Management:

The Index is designed to deliver exposure to equity securities of large capitalization U.S. issuers that engage in “just business behavior” based on (…) an annual ranking of issuers in the Russell 1000® Index (the “Reference Index”) based on their quantitative performance assessment of certain issue areas which, for the rankings published in 2024, were: how they treat their workers, their customers, the communities they interact with, the environment, and their shareholders.

The underlying index consists of the top-ranked 50% of companies in the Russell 1000 based on the most recent rankings. Constituents are weighted based on market capitalization, with an adjustment to keep the same industry weights as in the Russell 1000 at every annual reconstitution.

The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 9%. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

The portfolio is mostly invested in large and mega caps (about 81% of asset value), with a focus on technology (30.3%) and significant exposure in financials (14.8%).

It is quite concentrated, with significant exposure to a few companies. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 38.5% of asset value and the four heaviest positions are between 5% and 7%.

JUST is in fourth position for total return since August 2020 and third for Sharpe ratio, behind USXF and EFIV, which both have lower fees and higher trading volumes.

Takeaway

Goldman Sachs JUST U.S. Large Cap Equity ETF holds about half of Russell 1000 stocks based on a ranking system using ESG metrics. JUST is very close to its parent index in sector weights, fundamentals, return and risk. It is a good point: JUST provides a more ethical investing vehicle than the benchmark for investors who care about ethics, without harming performance. However, based on performance since August 2020, two other ESG funds look more compelling. From a methodological point of view, I find the industry-neutral weighting constraint debatable. If investing ethics is the real purpose, it makes sense to let some industries go underweight based on ethics-related metrics, like most other ESG funds do.

data.title: Wells Fargo: Higher Interest Rates Create Fixed Income Opportunities
data.description:
Introduction

Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) doesn’t need a long introduction, as this US-based financial institution enjoys a worldwide name and brand recognition. In my previous articles I focused on some of the bank’s preferred shares as I liked the risk/reward ratio of the non-cumulative preferred stock. As the interest rates on the financial markets continued to increase since my previous article was published, I think the risk/reward ratio has only gotten better as preferred shareholders can now lock in a preferred dividend yield of around 6.4%.

WFC only needed 6% of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends

When I look at the preferred stock of a bank, I’m mainly interested in checking the preferred dividend coverage ratio. While the asset coverage ratio usually also is an important element to me, if the bank’s asset coverage ratio suddenly gets substantially weaker, there will be bigger problems in the world to deal with than the preferred stock.

Looking at Q1 results, which are the most recently published results by Wells Fargo, the bank saw its total interest income decrease by almost $2B while its interest expenses decreased by just over $1.1B. This resulted in a total net interest income of $11.5B, a 6% decrease compared to the first quarter of last year.

Fortunately, this difference was almost fully compensated for by the lower non-interest expenses which decreased by approximately $450M on a YoY basis while the non-interest income remained relatively stable. This ultimately resulted in a pre-tax and pre loan loss provision income of almost $6.2B (compared to $6.6B in the first quarter of last year. But thanks to a lower tax bill, the net income increased to $4.8B and after taking the loss into account that was attributable to the non-controlling interests, the net profit attributable to the shareholders of Wells Fargo was approximately $4.9B. And as the income statement above shows, the bank needed just $278M to cover the preferred dividend expenses. This meant the net profit attributable to the common shareholders was $4.6B, for an EPS of $1.41.

But more importantly, from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, the bank only needed just under 6% of its attributable net profit to cover the preferred dividends.

The preferred shares are still attractive

In a previous article, I had a closer look at one of the bank’s series of preferred shares, as I liked the yield and the potential for capital gains if/when the interest rates on the financial markets come down. Preferred stock with a low fixed preferred dividend may provide the potential to generate capital gains as well, as share prices will increase when interest rates come down (assuming the risk premium remains unchanged).

The Series Z preferred shares (WFC.PR.Z) currently offer a fixed preferred dividend of $1.1875 per year for a 4.75% yield on the principal amount of $25/unit. But as you can see below, the stock isn’t even close to its $25 principal value, but is currently trading at approximately $18.54 per share.

This means the $1.1875 per year represents a preferred dividend yield of 6.4%. This represents a mark-up of approximately 235 bps to the five-year US Treasury yield (which currently stands at 4.04%), which, I think, is quite appropriate despite the non-cumulative nature of the preferred shares. The reputational damage to the bank would be massive if it would skip a preferred dividend payment, even if it's legally entitled to do so without repercussions.

Investment thesis

I agree with the other authors that argued the lifting of the asset cap is a positive change for Wells Fargo. That being said, I will be vigilant to ensure the bank’s total equity versus total asset ratio (and preferred equity vs. total equity ratio) remain acceptable. I do expect the preferred dividend coverage ratio to improve as the expanded asset base should result in higher earnings for Wells Fargo. Other than that, there should be no noticeable impact for the preferred shareholders of Wells Fargo, considering these securities are considered fixed income.

I think the risk/reward ratio offered by the bank’s preferred shares is excellent at the current preferred dividend yield of 6.4% (I already liked the almost 6% yield the last time I discussed these preferred shares, so the additional 10% discount to the share price is quite attractive).

I have a small long position in Wells Fargo’s Series Z preferred shares, and I also have a long position in (WFC.PR.L), a "busted" series of preferred shares, which currently offer a yield of just over 6.5%.

data.title: Amalgamated Financial: No Longer Attractively Priced Following The Tariff Announcements (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Due to recent developments in the economy, I’m reducing both my loan growth and net interest margin estimates for Amalgamated Financial Corp. (AMAL). In my last report, I adopted a buy rating and gave an EPS forecast of $3.50 for 2025. I’m now reducing my EPS estimate to $3.25. Further, I’m downgrading Amalgamated Financial to a hold rating because my valuation analysis shows that the stock is no longer attractively priced.

Headwinds For The C&I And Multi-Family Loan Segments In NYC

Amalgamated Financial’s loan portfolio grew by just 0.2% in the first quarter of 2025. The management mentioned in the conference call that the loan growth came on the back of the Commercial & Industrial (“C&I”) and the Multifamily loan segments. The management also mentioned that the commercial real estate, consumer solar loans, and residential loan segments were primarily in runoff mode. The management did not expect any growth in these three portfolios going forward. As shown below, these three segments make up large portions of the total loan book, so weakness in these segments can hurt overall loan growth.

Although the management appeared to be optimistic about the C&I segment, I’m not hopeful because of the tariffs and the trade policy uncertainty. An overwhelming majority of AMAL’s loans are based in New York City. As a national lender, the company also has sizable exposure to other urban markets, including Washington D.C., San Francisco, and Boston. The unemployment rates of these markets improved in the last quarter, as shown in the chart below.

However, the recent improvement does not mean that the tariffs will be a non-event for the labor market. It’s just going to take some time for the tariffs and counter-tariffs to affect the economy. The World Bank is projecting the country’s GDP growth rate to halve from 2.8% in 2024 to 1.4% in 2025, and then remain low at around 1.6% in 2026, as mentioned in the Global Economic Prospects June 2025 Report.

As for the multifamily segment, I’m expecting it to take a hit from the U.S. Government’s tariffs on steel and aluminum. As of the writing of this report, the U.S. has a 50% tariff on these materials from all countries except the UK (news source). The tariffs will raise input costs, leading to higher prices for renters/condo buyers and consequently lower demand.

Moreover, mass deportations can hurt the construction of multifamily buildings. According to a study by the University of Michigan, around 63% of New York City’s construction workers are immigrants, and an estimated 40% of those workers are undocumented.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan book to grow by just 1.7% in 2025. In my previous report on Amalgamated Financial, which I wrote in March 2025 before the global tariff announcement, I projected loan growth of 6.1% for this year. I’ve slashed my estimate because of the tariffs and their expected impact on the economy.

Deposit Outlook Remains Positive

Contrary to my loan outlook, my outlook on deposits remains satisfactory. During the first quarter, the deposit book surged by an impressive 3.2%. Amalgamated Financial’s deposit book has a lot of ebb and flow due to the large portion of political deposits. These deposits surged to $2.0 billion, representing 26% of total deposits, in 3Q-2024 due to the elections and then proceeded to fall to $1.0 billion, representing 14% of total deposits, in 4Q-2024, as per the details given in the presentation. The political accounts had already started getting replenished in the last quarter, and they’re likely to swell again by the mid-term elections next year.

The management mentioned in the presentation that it is targeting balance sheet growth of around 3% for this year. Keeping in mind the above factors and the first quarter’s impressive growth, I think the management will be able to grow deposits by ~ 6.4% in 2025. As I’m not expecting much loan growth, Amalgamated Financial will have to deploy the excess deposits into investment securities. The following table lays out my estimates for the key balance sheet line items.

Further Margin Decline Likely Due to Rate Cuts

Amalgamated Financial’s net interest margin dropped by 4 basis points in the first quarter of 2025, which was worse than my expectations. The margin will slip some more towards the end of 2025 because I’m expecting the Fed to cut the Fed funds rate by around 50 basis points in the second half of this year. Threats of an economic slowdown in the wake of tariff implementation will likely encourage the Fed to cut rates. The management’s rate-simulation model shows that a 100-basis points rate cut can have a material impact on the net interest income.

Meanwhile, the anticipated asset mix shift away from loans towards securities (that I discussed above) will barely have any effect on the margin. This is because the yields on loans and securities are currently quite similar. According to details given in the 10-Q filing, the average loan portfolio yield was 5.00% while the average securities portfolio yield was 5.15%.

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to drop by six basis points in the last three quarters of 2025. I’m expecting an average margin of 3.43% for 2025, down 8 basis points from last year’s level. Compared to my previous report on the company, I’ve reduced my margin estimate because of the first quarter’s worse-than-expected performance.

Expecting Earnings To Dip By 6% To $3.25

As I have reduced both my loan growth and net interest margin estimates, my earnings estimate will also decline. To arrive at my EPS estimate, I’m making the following additional assumptions.

  1. The provision expense for loan losses dropped unusually in the first quarter of this year. I’m expecting it to return to a normal level for the rest of the year.
  2. To forecast the non-interest income, I’m assuming most fee income will remain stable. Moreover, I’m taking the management’s guidance for solar tax credit investment income given in the presentation. The management expects this income to be NEGATIVE $3.2 million in 2025 because of depreciation in the early stage of the investment.
  3. I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to remain rangebound near the first quarter’s level of around 54%.

Overall, I’m expecting an EPS of $3.25 for 2025, which is 6% below last year’s EPS. Further, my new estimate is below my previous estimate of $3.50 because I’ve reduced my loan growth and margin estimates.

Downgrading To Hold

Amalgamated Financial’s stock price has risen by 2.4% since my last report on the company in March 2025, wherein I adopted a buy rating. Interestingly, the short interest on AMAL has shot up to record highs since my last report.

To update my valuation, I’m using the historical price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples.

Multiplying the average P/E ratio with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $29.8 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB ratio with my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $30.6 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $30.2, which is just 0.2% below the June 13 closing market price. Amalgamated Financial also offers a low dividend yield of 1.9%. Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of 1.6%. As this is very low, I’m downgrading Amalgamated Financial to a hold rating from my previous rating of buy.

To conclude, due to the recent macroeconomic developments mostly related to tariffs, I’m reducing my earnings estimate and downgrading Amalgamated Financial to hold.

data.title: Investors Watch Out For Core Retail Sales, With Accenture And Kroger Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages opened lower on Thursday, with investor sentiment weighed down by geopolitical risks and trade tensions. Despite a soft PPI data, headlines on rising geopolitical risks in the Middle East, and a renewed tariff threat by U.S. President Donald Trump, continued to alarm traders. Investors will keep an eye out for tariff announcements in the coming weeks, after Trump said he intended to send letters to trading partners, setting unilateral tariff rates.

Among trade remarks from Trump, investors will also be awaiting certain economic reports due next week, such as the OPEC Monthly Report on Monday, and Core retail sales, Export and Import Price Index, Retail Sales, and Industrial Production on Tuesday. Wednesday will include Housing Starts and Building Permits, while Jobless Claims will be released on Thursday. The week will end with reports on the Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index, and Baker Hughes Rig Count.

Accenture (ACN), Kroger (KR), CarMax (KMX), and Lennar (LEN) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, June 16: Lennar. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, June 17: Jabil Circuit (JBL), Kirklands (KIRK), Vince (VNCE). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, June 20: Accenture, Kroger, CarMax. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Global stock markets will be on edge as developments in the Middle East play out. New Fortress Energy (NFE) and CoreWeave (CRWV) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include SafeSpace Global (OTCPK:SSGC), Klotho Neurosciences (KLTO), and M3-Brigade Acquisition V. (MBAV). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include PG&E (PCG) and Copart (CPRT).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Meta Platforms (META), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Merck (MRK), Altria Group (MO), Amphenol (APH), Salesforce (CRM), Lam Research (LRCX), Best Buy (BBY), and Broadcom (AVGO). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: Amazon (AMZN)'s AWS Inforce conference will begin on Monday in Philadelphia. The three-day event will provide insights into securing workloads on AWS. Monday will also see the start of the Paris Air Show, which will see an opportunity for aerospace companies to unveil new technologies and announce strategic orders. Meanwhile, the three-day Marine Money Week event will begin in New York City on the same day. Wednesday will see the beginning of the three-day MWC Shanghai conference, which will showcase advancements in mobile technology.

Accenture Earnings Preview: Accenture is set to report its third quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting a revenue of $17.28B and earnings per share of $3.32. Analysts expect slight beats on revenue and EPS, but don't expect these to drive meaningful upside in the share price. Investors will be looking for sustained momentum in new bookings, especially in Gen AI. Ongoing margin pressures, macroeconomic risks, and federal contract uncertainties are keeping analysts cautious.

Kroger Earnings Preview: Kroger is scheduled to report its first quarter results next week. Analysts expect the company to report earnings per share of $1.45 on a revenue of $45.29B. Kroger said last month that it is adding 15,000 employees to its workforce for positions ranging from cashiers, baggers, pharmacy technicians, and delivery drivers. The decision to beef up its workforce comes as the grocery industry faces rising costs for food, labor, and a more cautious consumer.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Carriage Services: Further Upside Requires Further M&A (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Carriage Services, Inc. (CSV) has still been quiet on the M&A front. The funeral home and cemetery operator has moderated its debt through non-core asset divestments and by using internal cash flow on debt payments, creating a better financial position for further acquisitions. Yet, such transactions haven't been announced so far. Carriage Services’ own operations remain stable.

The stock has returned 10% after my previous January article, compared to the S&P 500’s lower return of 1%. The article was titled “Carriage Services: M&A To Resume In 2025 Amid Growing Cemetery Preneed Demand”. I remained at a Buy rating in the article.

Carriage Services Still Hasn’t Resumed M&A

Carriage Services’ M&A strategy still hasn’t been resumed with any finalized transactions. The company hasn’t announced any funeral home or cemetery acquisition with nearly half of 2025 already behind; while Carriage Services is eyeing a return to acquisitions for the ongoing year, such a shift isn’t seen yet.

With the company’s history of accretive acquisitions, I believe that the lack of transactions is disappointing. Although Carriage Services' debt remains high at $521 million, the 4.2 leverage ratio is still manageable for a highly stable and predictable business like Carriage Services operates – I don’t believe the balance sheet to be too weak for acquisitions. The company has still generated $44.1 million in levered free cash flow over the past twelve months.

On top of the lack of acquisitions, Carriage Services has even divested some further non-core assets, gaining $18.7 million in cash proceeds in Q1 but losing $2.3 million in EBITDA at an approximate 8.1 EV/EBITDA. The company is also looking at potential additional asset divestments to refocus on higher-value assets; for now, Carriage Services’ M&A is even shrinking the company’s footprint.

The Q1 earnings call still reiterated the point that Carriage Services is looking at acquisition candidates, and noted that the company is already in discussions with a number of owners. The notations suggest that Carriage Services may finally return to acquisitions in the latter parts of 2025. The lack of transactions in 2025 so far has still been disappointing and somewhat lowers my expectations of Carriage Services’ future M&A pace, though.

The Earnings Outlook Remains Stable

As can be expected, Carriage Services’ own performance has stayed quite stable. The first quarter showed total revenues of $107.1 million, up by 3.5% year-on-year. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA came in at $32.9 million, showing strong leverage from $27.0 million a year ago. Both revenues and the adjusted EPS beat Wall Street's consensus.

Carriage Services’ funeral performance was especially good. Sold funeral contracts grew by 2.4% to 12055, and average revenue per contract grew by 2.2% to $5731 with solid pricing power, creating 4.6% growth for the segment to total revenues of $69.1 million. With strong cost discipline, the funeral segment’s EBITDA also grew very well to $29.5 million at a 42.8% margin, showing 140 basis points of year-on-year margin expansion.

The cemetery segment had a more mixed performance in Q1 – sold preneed interment rights declined by -4.4% to 3100, being a notable shift from Carriage Services’ previous strong growth in preneed demand. Carriage Services’ preneed contract growth a strong 10.1% increase in the average price still drove 5.8% revenue growth for the segment to a total of $27.9 million. The cemetery segment’s EBITDA even declined slightly to $11.4 million.

Overall, Carriage Services’ outlook stands fair. Revenues are guided at $400-410 million for 2025, suggesting organic growth of approximately 2.2% at the mid-point. Adjusted EBITDA is guided at $128-133 million, up from $126.2 million in 2024. Revenue growth from cemetery preneeds is expected to reaccelerate after a weak first quarter, creating a foundation for fairly good total organic earnings growth.

Carriage Services’ Valuation Has Reached a Fair Level

I updated my discounted cash flow [DCF] model for Carriage Services. I still assume organic growth of 1.5% over the long term, still aided by M&A in the next few years. I now estimate a total 2024-2034 CAGR of 2.4%, suggesting 0.9 percentage points of annual revenue growth from acquisitions over the period. The acquisitions are weighed to the next few years. I previously assumed more aggressive acquisitions in the DCF model.

I now estimate the EBIT margin to reach a 21.5% level through Project Trinity savings, expecting slightly less margin expansion than previously. I have slightly raised my cash flow conversion estimates as I have lowered my expectations for Carriage Services’ acquisitions.

The estimates put Carriage Services’ fair value at $51.09, 16% above the stock price at the time of writing. The stock still has some upside, but I believe that the valuation’s attractiveness is fading. Lower acquisition activity creates less long-term upside, and the upside now only represents a fair margin of safety in my opinion. Carriage Services’ valuation has reached a fair level.

The fair value estimate is up slightly from $49.18 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.03%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 5.61% interest rate, annualized from Q1 interest expenses and debt. I still estimate Carriage Services’ debt to remain high at a 60% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.41% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.88. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 8.72% and the WACC at 7.03%.

Takeaway

Carriage Services still hasn't announced any new acquisitions, being a slight disappointment as the company's financial position is already much healthier. Operationally, Carriage Services' outlook remains fair after cemetery preneed growth had a hiccup in Q1. The stock has reached a fair valuation, and as such, I downgrade my rating on Carriage Services to Hold.

data.title: Collect Double-Digit Yields From Niche Fixed-Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I recall when I worked in a financial institution, we had one elderly customer who would visit every month, the day after our new lending and certificate of deposit rates were published by our head office. She would come in and check on our current CD rates. She always complained that the available CD rates were significantly lower than they used to be decades ago and that she couldn't get much return on her money within the bank. It made sense at the time because rates were low. If the bank was lending money out cheaply, it wasn't going to pay top dollar for money to be deposited there.

As an investor in the current market, I am always looking for wonderful income opportunities, much like this elderly customer, to find great income from lower-risk investments to enjoy. I want my money to earn top dollar for me, no matter where I invest it, and I refuse to have a holding in my portfolio that doesn't pay me top dollar.

Today, I want to explore niche fixed-income sector opportunities that we can leverage through CEFs (Closed-End Funds) to access. I want to show you an opportunity that will allow you to earn top dollar on your money from financial institutions without having to put it into a CD. I also want to show you another opportunity that maximizes the distressed bond space, allowing you to unlock great income in the process.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: BANX - Yield 10%

Have you ever wondered why banks are so willing to lend to people, everyday consumers who seem to have very little financial education or financial knowledge, and yet financial institutions continually lend money to these individuals, often reaping a profit in the process? How do they always feel comfortable being able to do this?

Part of the answer comes down to the amount of money that they lend out. The base assumption for any lending situation is that there is a 2% default rate - out of every hundred people you lend money to, one is not going to pay for an unforeseen situation, and the other will have lied to you at some point and isn't going to repay you. If you start seeing default rates well above 2%, you're entering into a much riskier realm, or you have poor underwriting standards; below 2%, and eventually, you're going to have a reversion back to the mean.

Financial institutions use various tools to reduce their regulatory risk levels while maintaining strong customer relationships. One of these tools is to package a large number of consumer loans together and sell off a portion of the risk to a third party. It allows an institution to maintain customer relationships, recycle their capital, and reduce their risk. The people who buy into those securities enjoy strong returns from a wide range of various consumer loans; they don't have to worry about collecting the money, and they simply get paid their portion. This niche type of security is called regulatory capital relief security and is usually only accessible to ultra-high net-worth individuals or other types of financial institutions. ArrowMark Financial Corp (BANX) is a CEF that focuses on this niche opportunity. It allows you to have access to something that very few people would be able to access and receive a strong income. Over the lifespan of the fund, they have been able to maintain strong distribution coverage, collecting well more than they pay out, and maintaining their NAV over the last 7 years, even as rates spiked and settled again. Source

As rates have fallen, we would expect that their income will drop. However, due to their strong coverage, we do not foresee any need to reduce their regular distribution. We do expect the special distributions to be reduced or disappear altogether.

You can collect double-digit yields while accepting income from massive financial institutions. Instead of paying the bank, let them pay you!

Pick #2: PTY - Yield 10.3%

In recent years, we've witnessed the most aggressive rate-hiking cycle since the 1980s, and as a result, we've seen debt of all sorts experience significant price declines.

Typically, we see debt prices crash when default rates and loss rates are high - which makes sense, if borrowers are defaulting, owning debt is less attractive. Yet, over the past few years, we've seen extremely low default rates across all sorts of debt investments. Among many types of debt, default rates have been well below average and even at all-time lows. However, those strong fundamentals did not stop prices from declining to levels we frequently see during a crisis.

In our view, this is a generational opportunity to be buying debt of all stripes. However, it isn't always easy for retail investors to buy all types of debt. The world of lending comprises huge sharks moving around substantial sums of money. It is a world where it is very difficult for retail investors to compete.

PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) has been providing retail investors access to this world for 23 years. PTY invests in a wide variety of debt investments, including agency MBS, non-agency MBS, high-yield corporate bonds, developed and emerging market foreign bonds, and more: Source

An investor could spend a lifetime becoming an expert on just a few of these investment classes, or an investor could invest in PTY and let the PIMCO team navigate these waters.

PIMCO has demonstrated time and again that it has the ability to identify value opportunities - often buying where others fear to tread. When the housing market was collapsing, PIMCO was buying up mortgages, which are still in PTY's portfolio today.

When the market feared Carvana (CVNA) would be filing for bankruptcy, PIMCO bought up a significant portion of its debt and negotiated a debt exchange agreement. The result was a significant-realized gain for PTY as it bought the debt below par, and has sold approximately 75% of its position since September 2024 at a premium to par.

When Windstream filed for bankruptcy, PIMCO stepped in and bought a position, navigating through the bankruptcy process, held the debt while Windstream was a private company, and is in the process of realizing a gain exiting that position.

Most recently, PTY has been taking advantage of the disruption of international markets during the trade war, approximately doubling its holdings of foreign government bonds since September.

When there is panic in a debt market, PIMCO is one of the names you see stepping in and looking to buy value. They don't have a perfect batting average. We remember when the floor fell out on PIMCO's bet on Argentina bonds, which was one of their larger losses. Then again, nobody with experience has a perfect batting average.

What PTY has shown is that for over 22 years, it can navigate the debt markets and provide investors with a hefty dividend along the way.

Conclusion

BANX and PTY provide us with wonderful opportunities to access markets that the average investor would not be able to participate in. The average retail investor, or even the experienced individual investor, would not have the capability or capital to readily invest in the sectors that BANX and PTY allow us to access. By leveraging skilled portfolio managers, we're able to collect wonderful income and returns from sectors that otherwise would be completely shut off to us. It also allows us not to have to actively participate in the daily activities required to collect income from these sectors, offloading that responsibility to the portfolio managers while creating a stream of passive income in return.

Retirement should be a time when you engage in the hobbies and activities that you enjoy the most. For most of us, it's not going to be trading in banking securities or distressed debt. If it is, that's wonderful! But if, like me, you prefer the simpler things in life, like a hot cup of coffee on a porch swing with your spouse or taking your dog for a walk and enjoying the sounds of the birds chirping as the sun rises, then buying a CEF that enables you to collect passive income from the hard work of others is perfect for you. It's the kind of investment we hold hundreds of within High Dividend Opportunities.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.
